params.text:
What you might need to raise

Whistleblowing covers serious wrongdoing that harms, or risks harming, the people you support, your colleagues or the public. It is broader than a single act of poor care. If something feels seriously wrong, it is better to raise it than to assume someone else already has.

Concerns worth raising include:

  • Poor care: neglect, people left in pain, or care given without dignity.
  • Abuse: physical, sexual, emotional, financial or discriminatory harm.
  • Unsafe practice: dangerous staffing levels, missed medication, or ignored procedures.

Wrongdoing beyond the bedside

Not every concern is about hands-on care. Whistleblowing also covers dishonesty and danger in how a service is run.

  • Fraud: false records, stolen money, or claiming for care that was never given.
  • Cover-ups: hiding a mistake, altering notes, or silencing anyone who complains.
  • Health and safety dangers: broken equipment, fire hazards or serious infection risks.

You do not need proof, and you do not need to be certain. If you have a reasonable, honest belief that something is wrong, that is enough to raise it — investigating is someone else’s job, not yours.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about what you can raise.
params.textField: Whistleblowing covers dishonesty such as *fraud*, where money or records are falsified. Hiding a mistake or altering notes is a *cover-up*. You do not need *proof* before you raise a concern. A reasonable, honest *belief* that something is wrong is enough to speak up. Broken equipment and fire hazards are *safety* dangers worth reporting.
